title: Brads cheat sheet
summary: Master exam-thinking cheat sheet: how to dissect any question, the keyword→answer map, the high-yield traps, and quick-reference theory, formulas and contract/risk/schedule rules.

sections.heading: How to dissect any question (do this every time)
sections.body:
Run this checklist on every question:

- Read the LAST sentence first. What is it actually asking — what should the PM do FIRST, NEXT, BEST, or what is MOST important? The call word changes the answer.
- Classify the situation: predictive / agile / hybrid? planning / executing / monitoring & controlling / closing? a current problem (issue) or a future uncertainty (risk)? a role question (who should act?) or a process question (what comes next?).
- Scan for keyword triggers (next section).
- Eliminate aggressively: anything that bypasses formal process (especially touching a baseline), jumps to "escalate to the sponsor" or "remove the person", or has the PM deciding for an agile team, is usually wrong. So is anything passive ("just log it / just monitor / just inform") when action is needed.
- Choose the option that is proactive, follows the correct process/order, and gives authority to the right role.
sections.tip: "First / Next" rewards the correct process ORDER and the least-drastic correct action. "Best / Most appropriate" rewards the option that addresses the root cause through proper process.

sections.heading: Agile vs predictive vs hybrid — read the signals
sections.body:
Spotting the delivery approach tells you the flavour of the right answer.

- Agile signals: self-organising team, sprint, iteration, user story, backlog, Product Owner, daily standup, increment. Correct-answer flavour: empower the team, diagnose WITH the team, support the PO, inspect-and-adapt.
- Predictive signals: baseline, WBS, change request, CCB, critical path, earned value. Correct-answer flavour: follow formal process, submit a change request, respect the plan.
- Hybrid signals: mixes both, or mentions tailoring. Correct-answer flavour: fit the tool to each part — structure where you need control, flexibility where you need learning.
sections.tip: Sprints / backlog / PO → lean Agile. Baselines / WBS / CCB / critical path → lean Predictive. Mixes both or "tailoring" → Hybrid. Don't apply pure agile or pure predictive to everything.

sections.heading: The keyword → move map (memorise this)
sections.body:
A large share of the exam is pattern-matching these triggers to the right move:

- "Baseline" / "approved schedule/cost/scope" → raise a Change Request (Integrated Change Control).
- "Has happened / is happening / was pulled / is delaying" → Issue Log.
- "May / could / potential / in the future" → Risk Register.
- "New stakeholder / new role appeared" → update the Stakeholder Engagement (Management) Plan.
- "Team is struggling / missing milestones / bored" → meet and diagnose WITH the team (training, conflict, skills) before changing timelines or adding rewards.
- "Value is in doubt / long timeline / may not meet needs" → build a prototype or MVP to show value.
- "Functional manager pulled/took the resource" → talk to the functional manager FIRST to get them back.
- "Don't know current vs desired engagement" → Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix.
- "Stakeholders not attending meetings" → reach out to them individually to understand why.
- "Delays in implementing risk responses" → missing or unclear Risk Owners.
- "Keep projects aligned with business objectives across the org" → PMO.
- "High-risk user stories" → schedule them in EARLIER iterations.
sections.tip: Drill these until they're reflexes. On a close question, find the trigger phrase first — it usually decides the answer.

sections.heading: Issue vs Risk — now vs later
sections.body:
The single most repeated distinction. Ask: has it happened yet?

- Issue = a current, realised problem (it is happening now) → Issue Log.
- Risk = a future uncertain event (it might happen) → Risk Register.

The classic trap is treating a current, realised event as a future risk.
sections.tip: "Has it happened?" Yes → Issue Log. No / uncertain → Risk Register.

sections.heading: Plan vs Register — the document trap
sections.body:
Distractors constantly swap the Plan and the Register, for both risk and stakeholders.

- A Plan describes HOW you'll do something — the strategy, methodology, roles, thresholds. Created (mostly) once during planning.
  - Risk Management Plan = how risk will be run.
  - Stakeholder Engagement Plan = how you'll engage each stakeholder.
- A Register is the living LIST — the actual items, updated continuously.
  - Risk Register = the individual risks, analysis, owners, responses.
  - Stakeholder Register = the people, roles, interest/influence.

When the engagement STRATEGY or risk APPROACH changes, you update the Plan — not just the Register.
sections.tip: Strategy / approach / how-we-engage → the PLAN. A specific item or person → the REGISTER.

sections.heading: Risk management — the highest-yield area
sections.body:
Know the process order cold:
1. Plan Risk Management — defines HOW risk will be managed.
2. Identify Risks — create the Risk Register (high-level first).
3. Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis — prioritise by probability × impact.
4. Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis — model the numeric effect (not always done).
5. Plan Risk Responses — develop the response actions (the "risk action plan").
6. Implement Risk Responses — owners carry them out.
7. Monitor Risks — track residual/secondary risks, watch for new ones, check response effectiveness.

Response strategies:
- Threats: Escalate, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
- Opportunities: Escalate, Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.

Rules: identify before you respond; when a new initiative or worry appears, identify high-level risks first (don't quantify yet); each response gets a Risk Owner who implements it; if something already identified as a risk needs handling, review the Risk Management Plan's strategy first.
sections.tip: New risk situation → identify/assess first, never jump to a number or a solution. "Risk action plan" = Plan Risk Responses, not the overall Risk Management Plan.

sections.heading: Risk numbers & reserves (expansion)
sections.body:
Often-confused risk concepts the exam loves:

- EMV (Expected Monetary Value) = Probability × Impact. Sum the EMVs of identified risks to size contingency.
- Contingency reserve = for KNOWN (identified) risks. It sits INSIDE the cost/schedule baseline and the PM can use it.
- Management reserve = for UNKNOWN (unidentified) risks. It sits OUTSIDE the baseline and needs management approval to release.
- Secondary risk = a new risk created BY a response you implemented.
- Residual risk = the risk that REMAINS after a response.
- Risk appetite / tolerance / threshold = how much risk the organisation will accept; thresholds are set in the Risk Management Plan.
sections.tip: Known risk → contingency reserve (in the baseline, PM controls). Unknown risk → management reserve (outside the baseline, needs approval).

sections.heading: Change control & baselines
sections.body:
Once scope, schedule or cost is baselined you cannot edit it directly. Any baseline change runs through Perform Integrated Change Control: raise a formal Change Request → assess impact across all constraints → CCB decides → only then update the baseline and affected documents.

Many documents update freely WITHOUT a change request: Stakeholder Register, Risk Register, Issue Log, Assumption Log, Lessons Learned Register.

Configuration management controls the product's specs/versions; change control governs changes to the plan. Even a longer-than-expected procurement lead time that shifts the schedule needs a Change Request.
sections.tip: "Update / revise the baseline" → Change Request, almost every time. Logs and registers are fair game to update without one.

sections.heading: Roles & authority
sections.body:
Keep these straight — the exam confuses them on purpose:

- Project Manager — delivers the project day to day; in agile, facilitates and removes impediments.
- Sponsor — funds and champions ONE project, owns the business case, removes high-level obstacles.
- PMO (Project/Program Management Office) — sets standards, methods and governance, and can be a key decision-maker keeping projects aligned to business objectives ACROSS the organisation.
- Product Owner — owns the product, the backlog and its priority, and what gets released.
- Functional Manager — owns the people/resources in a matrix org.

Program = a group of related projects managed together for benefits you can't get separately. Portfolio = all projects/programs/operations aligned to strategy.
sections.tip: "Aligns/standardises across projects" = PMO. "Funds & champions THIS project" = Sponsor. Confusing the two is the classic trap.

sections.heading: PM authority by org structure (expansion)
sections.body:
How much power the PM has depends on the structure:

- Functional — PM has little/no authority; the functional manager runs the show. PM is often a coordinator.
- Weak matrix — PM is a coordinator/expediter; limited authority.
- Balanced matrix — authority shared between PM and functional manager.
- Strong matrix — PM has moderate-to-high authority; resources still report functionally.
- Projectized — PM has high/full authority; the team reports to the PM.
sections.tip: More "matrix/functional" in the scenario → the PM must negotiate with functional managers for people, rather than direct them.

sections.heading: Leadership styles (expansion)
sections.body:
- Directive (Directing) — task and results focused; tells the team what and how. Good for crises, inexperienced teams, or when structure is needed.
- Servant leadership — serves the team, removes impediments, builds the environment. The agile default.
- Situational — adapt the style to the team's maturity: Directing → Coaching → Supporting → Delegating.
- Transactional — motivates through rewards and penalties tied to performance.
- Transformational — inspires through vision and growth.
- Laissez-faire — hands-off; suits a highly capable, self-directed team (but risky if they're not).
sections.tip: Agile / self-organising team → servant leadership. Crisis or brand-new team → directive. Match the style to the team's maturity.

sections.heading: Motivation theories (expansion)
sections.body:
High-yield and easily confused:

- Maslow — hierarchy of needs (physiological → safety → social → esteem → self-actualisation); lower needs first.
- Herzberg — hygiene factors (salary, conditions, policy) only PREVENT dissatisfaction; motivators (achievement, recognition, growth) drive satisfaction.
- McGregor — Theory X (people dislike work, need control) vs Theory Y (people are self-motivated, seek responsibility).
- McClelland — needs for Achievement, Affiliation, Power.
- Vroom's Expectancy — motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence: effort → performance → a reward the person actually values.
- Ouchi — Theory Z: long-term employment and loyalty drive productivity.
sections.tip: "Effort leads to a reward they value" = Expectancy. "Pay/conditions don't motivate, they only stop dissatisfaction" = Herzberg.

sections.heading: Conflict & team development (expansion)
sections.body:
Conflict modes (Thomas-Kilmann):
- Collaborate / Problem-solve — confront the issue with those involved for a win/win. The PMP-preferred mode in most cases.
- Compromise — both give something up (lose/lose).
- Smooth / Accommodate — emphasise agreement, downplay differences.
- Force / Direct — impose a solution (win/lose); fast but damages relationships; for emergencies.
- Withdraw / Avoid — retreat; only for trivial issues or to cool down.

Most project conflict is over schedules, priorities and resources — not personalities. Tuckman's stages: Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning (a re-formed team can regress).
sections.tip: When in doubt, pick Collaborate/Confront — address the root cause directly with the people involved. Avoid/Force are rarely the best answer.

sections.heading: Stakeholder engagement
sections.body:
- Plan vs Register: update the Engagement (Management) Plan when roles or strategies change; the Register holds the who.
- Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix — maps each stakeholder's CURRENT vs DESIRED engagement (Unaware → Resistant → Neutral → Supportive → Leading). Use it when you don't know the gap.
- Power/Interest grid — high/high = Manage Closely; high power/low interest = Keep Satisfied; low power/high interest = Keep Informed; low/low = Monitor.
- Salience model — Power, Legitimacy, Urgency (all three = Definitive, top priority).
- Stakeholders not attending → reach out individually to understand why; don't just escalate or cancel.
sections.tip: Engagement is tailored per stakeholder — and that tailoring lives in the PLAN, not the register.

sections.heading: Procurement & contracts (expansion)
sections.body:
Risk shifts with contract type:

- Fixed Price — seller carries the most risk. FFP (firm fixed); FPIF (incentive); FPEPA (economic price adjustment) for long-term/international deals with inflation or currency risk.
- Cost-Reimbursable — buyer carries the most risk. CPFF (fixed fee); CPIF (incentive fee); CPAF (award fee).
- Time & Material (T&M) — medium risk; good for staff augmentation or small/uncertain scope.

Transfer risk (e.g. penalty clauses, warranties) → contract or insurance. Make-or-buy compares building vs buying. For FPIF, the Point of Total Assumption (PTA) is where the seller starts absorbing all extra cost.
sections.tip: Well-defined scope → Fixed Price. Unclear/evolving scope → Cost-Reimbursable or T&M. Long international + inflation → FPEPA. Transfer a specific risk → insurance/contract clause.

sections.heading: Schedule (expansion)
sections.body:
- Critical path = the LONGEST path through the network; it has zero float and drives the project duration. With two independent paths, the critical one is the longer.
- Float/slack: total float = how long an activity can slip without delaying the project end; free float = without delaying its successor. Critical path activities have zero total float.
- Compression: Crashing = add resources to the critical path (costs money). Fast-tracking = run sequential work in parallel (adds risk/rework). Both only help on the critical path.
- Leads (overlap a successor) and lags (insert a wait).
- Resource leveling can extend the schedule; resource smoothing stays within float.
- A longer-than-planned procurement lead time still needs a Change Request to move the baseline.
sections.tip: Two paths → critical = longest. Need it shorter: Crash = money, Fast-track = risk. Only the critical path matters for either.

sections.heading: Estimating & earned value — quick reference (expansion)
sections.body:
Estimating methods:
- Analogous — top-down from a similar past project; fast, cheap, least accurate.
- Parametric — rate × quantity (e.g. cost per unit); accurate if the data is good.
- Bottom-up — sum the detailed pieces; most accurate, most effort.
- Three-point (PERT) — tE = (O + 4M + P) / 6; range/standard deviation ≈ (P − O) / 6.

Earned Value (memorise the signs):
- PV planned, EV earned, AC actual.
- Schedule Variance SV = EV − PV; Cost Variance CV = EV − AC. Negative = behind / over.
- SPI = EV / PV; CPI = EV / AC. Below 1 = behind / over.
- EAC = BAC / CPI (when the current rate continues); VAC = BAC − EAC.
sections.tip: Variance = EV minus (PV or AC). Index = EV over (PV or AC). Anything negative or below 1 is bad. Schedule uses PV; cost uses AC.

sections.heading: Quality — quick reference (expansion)
sections.body:
- Cost of Quality: conformance costs (Prevention + Appraisal — building it right and checking) vs nonconformance costs (Internal + External Failure — fixing/escaping defects). Investing in prevention is cheaper than paying for failure.
- Control limits vs specification limits: control limits are the process's own voice (typically ±3σ, set by the process); specification limits come from the customer. A point inside spec can still be out of control, and vice-versa.
- Rule of seven: seven consecutive points on one side of the mean signals a non-random pattern to investigate — even with no limit breached.
- Prevention over inspection; quality is planned in, not inspected in.
sections.tip: Prevention (conformance) beats failure (nonconformance) on cost. Control limits = the process; spec limits = the customer.

sections.heading: Common wrong-answer patterns — eliminate fast
sections.body:
On a close call, strike out options that:

- Bypass formal process — especially editing a baseline without a Change Request.
- Have the PM deciding for a self-organising agile team.
- Jump straight to "escalate to the sponsor" or "remove/replace the person".
- Are passive ("just inform", "just monitor", "just log it") when action is needed.
- Treat a current, realised problem as a future risk.
- Update the wrong artifact (the Register when it should be the Plan).
- Ignore the correct process order, or jump to quantification before identification.
- Assume the team needs rewards when the real issue is support, skills or conflict.
sections.tip: If an option does any of these, it's probably the distractor — even if it sounds reasonable.

sections.heading: When in doubt — the mindset table
sections.body:
- Baseline needs changing → Change Request (not modify directly).
- Current problem → Issue Log (not Risk Register).
- Future uncertainty → Risk Register (not Issue Log).
- Agile team struggling → diagnose WITH the team (not PM decides / change the timeline).
- Value questioned → Prototype / MVP (not extend the timeline or just log it).
- Resource pulled by a functional manager → talk to the functional manager first (not analyse impact first).
- New stakeholder / role → update the Stakeholder Engagement Plan (not the register only).
- Organisation-wide alignment → PMO (not the Sponsor).
- High-risk items in agile → address EARLIER (not later).
- Conflict → collaborate/confront the root cause (not avoid or force).
sections.tip: These cover a large fraction of the exam. If you can recall the left column from the right-hand cue, you'll bank easy marks.

sections.heading: Exam-day reminders
sections.body:
- Read the LAST sentence first, then the scenario.
- "First / next / best" → think process order and the least-drastic correct action.
- Agile/hybrid questions → favour empowerment, team diagnosis and Product Owner authority.
- Predictive questions → favour formal process and Change Requests for baselines.
- Eliminate answers that are passive, skip process, or put the wrong person in charge.
- Manage the clock — flag and move on; don't sink five minutes into one item.
- Trust the PMP mindset: proactive, servant-leader, value-driven, process-respecting.
sections.tip: Most ties break the same way: the proactive option that follows process and empowers the right role wins.
